examples.explanation: Input array: [2, 3, 5], n=3. Total subsets: 2^3 = 8. Every pair among {2,3,5} has gcd 1, so all subsets are pairwise coprime. Counting empty subset as valid gives final count = 8.

Input array: [2, 4, 6], n=3. Subsets: 2^3=8. Check each: 1. mask=000 (empty): if counted, coprime=true, but assume not counted per above. 2. mask=001: elements=[2], coprime=true, count=1. 3. mask=010: elements=[4], coprime=true, count=2. 4. mask=011: elements=[2,4], gcd(2,4)=2≠1, coprime=false, count=2. 5. mask=100: elements=[6], coprime=true, count=3. 6. mask=101: elements=[2,6], gcd(2,6)=2≠1, coprime=false, count=3. 7. mask=110: elements=[4,6], gcd(4,6)=2≠1, coprime=false, count=3. 8. mask=111: elements=[2,4,6], check pairs: gcd(2,4)=2≠1, so coprime=false, count=3. But output is 4? Re-evaluate: Single-element subsets are always coprime. So subsets: [2], [4], [6] are valid. Also, empty subset if counted? If empty counted, that's 4. So output=4 includes empty subset. Adjust trace: count empty subset. 1. mask=000: coprime=true, count=1. 2. mask=001: count=2. 3. mask=010: count=3. 4. mask=011: false. 5. mask=100: count=4. 6. mask=101: false. 7. mask=110: false. 8. mask=111: false. Final count=4.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(2^n * n^2)
approaches.summary: Generate all subsets using bitmask enumeration, check each subset for pairwise coprimality by verifying gcd of all pairs is 1. Use this for small input sizes (n <= 15) due to exponential growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Coprime Subsets Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Generate all subsets of an array and count those where all elements are pairwise coprime.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting all subsets of an array where every pair of elements in the subset has a greatest common divisor (GCD) of 1, meaning they are coprime. The brute force approach is straightforward but computationally expensive: enumerate all possible subsets using bitmask representation, then for each subset, check if all pairs are coprime by computing their GCDs. The intuition is that for small input sizes (n ≤ 15), the exponential 2^n subsets are manageable, and we can afford the O(n²) pairwise checks per subset. The algorithm works by iterating over all bitmasks from 0 to 2^n - 1, where each bitmask represents a subset (bit i set means element i is included). For each subset, extract the elements, then verify coprimality by checking GCD for every pair. If all pairs are coprime, increment the count. Time complexity is O(2^n * n²) due to subset enumeration and pairwise GCD checks; space complexity is O(n) for storing the current subset. This approach is foundational for understanding subset generation and coprime properties but is impractical for larger n due to exponential growth. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle empty subsets (which are trivially coprime and should be counted), incorrect bitmask iteration ranges, or inefficient GCD computations. The connection to higher-level approaches is that this brute force method serves as a baseline for optimization techniques like dynamic programming or inclusion-exclusion.

examples.trace: Input array: [2, 3, 5], n=3. Enumerate all 8 masks from 000 to 111. Every non-empty subset has pairwise gcd 1, and empty subset is also valid. Total coprime subsets = 8.

Input array: [2, 4, 6], n=3. Subsets: 2^3=8. Check each: 1. mask=000 (empty): if counted, coprime=true, but assume not counted per above. 2. mask=001: elements=[2], coprime=true, count=1. 3. mask=010: elements=[4], coprime=true, count=2. 4. mask=011: elements=[2,4], gcd(2,4)=2≠1, coprime=false, count=2. 5. mask=100: elements=[6], coprime=true, count=3. 6. mask=101: elements=[2,6], gcd(2,6)=2≠1, coprime=false, count=3. 7. mask=110: elements=[4,6], gcd(4,6)=2≠1, coprime=false, count=3. 8. mask=111: elements=[2,4,6], check pairs: gcd(2,4)=2≠1, so coprime=false, count=3. But output is 4? Re-evaluate: Single-element subsets are always coprime. So subsets: [2], [4], [6] are valid. Also, empty subset if counted? If empty counted, that's 4. So output=4 includes empty subset. Adjust trace: count empty subset. 1. mask=000: coprime=true, count=1. 2. mask=001: count=2. 3. mask=010: count=3. 4. mask=011: false. 5. mask=100: count=4. 6. mask=101: false. 7. mask=110: false. 8. mask=111: false. Final count=4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check if a Subset is Pairwise Coprime
ladders.desc: Given a list of integers, determine if every pair has GCD equal to 1.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core verification step in the brute force approach: for a given subset of numbers, check if all pairs of elements are coprime, i.e., their greatest common divisor (GCD) is 1. The intuition is that coprimality is a pairwise property; a subset is coprime if and only if for every two distinct elements, their GCD is 1. The algorithm iterates over all pairs (i, j) where i < j, computes GCD using the Euclidean algorithm, and returns false if any GCD is not 1. If all pairs pass, it returns true. Time complexity is O(k² * log(max(a,b))) where k is the subset size, since each GCD computation takes O(log(min(a,b))) time, but typically simplified to O(k²) for analysis. Space complexity is O(1) as it uses constant extra space. This sub-routine connects to the full problem by being called for each generated subset. Common mistakes include not handling empty or single-element subsets (which are trivially coprime), inefficient GCD implementation, or incorrect pair iteration leading to missed checks. The real code pattern involves nested loops and a GCD helper function.

examples.trace: Subset: [2, 3, 5], size k=3. Check pairs: 1. pair (2,3): gcd(2,3)=1. 2. pair (2,5): gcd(2,5)=1. 3. pair (3,5): gcd(3,5)=1. All GCDs are 1, so return true.
Subset: [2, 4, 6], size k=3. Check pairs: 1. pair (2,4): gcd(2,4)=2 ≠ 1, so return false immediately. No need to check other pairs.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Generate All Subsets Using Bitmask Enumeration
ladders.desc: Enumerate all possible subsets of an array by iterating over bitmasks from 0 to 2^n - 1.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the technique of generating all subsets of an array using bitmask representation, which is central to the brute force approach. The intuition is that each subset of an n-element array can be uniquely represented by an n-bit binary number (bitmask), where bit i is 1 if the i-th element is included, and 0 otherwise. By iterating over all integers from 0 to 2^n - 1, we cover every possible subset. For each mask, we extract the corresponding elements by checking which bits are set. The algorithm involves a loop from 0 to (1 << n) - 1, and for each mask, iterate through bits to build the subset list. Time complexity is O(2^n * n) for generation, and space complexity is O(n) for the temporary subset list. This connects to the full problem by providing the subsets to check for coprimality. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in the loop range, incorrect bit checking, or inefficient subset construction. The real code pattern uses bitwise operations like (mask >> i) & 1 to test bits.

examples.trace: Array: [2, 3], n=2. Masks from 0 to 3: 1. mask=00 (binary): bits: 0→exclude 2, 1→exclude 3, subset=[]. 2. mask=01: bits: 0→include 2, 1→exclude 3, subset=[2]. 3. mask=10: bits: 0→exclude 2, 1→include 3, subset=[3]. 4. mask=11: bits: 0→include 2, 1→include 3, subset=[2,3]. All subsets generated.
Array: [5], n=1. Masks from 0 to 1: 1. mask=0: bit 0→exclude 5, subset=[]. 2. mask=1: bit 0→include 5, subset=[5]. All subsets generated.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Greatest Common Divisor (GCD) Using Euclidean Algorithm
ladders.desc: Calculate the GCD of two integers using the recursive Euclidean algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the Euclidean algorithm for computing the greatest common divisor (GCD) of two integers, which is essential for checking coprimality. The intuition is based on the property that gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b), reducing the problem recursively until b becomes 0, at which point the GCD is a. The algorithm works by repeatedly replacing the larger number with the remainder of their division until one number is zero. Step-by-step: if b is 0, return a; otherwise, return gcd(b, a % b). Time complexity is O(log(min(a, b))) as the numbers reduce quickly. Space complexity is O(log(min(a, b))) for recursion depth, or O(1) for iterative implementation. This connects to higher levels by being used in pairwise GCD checks. Common mistakes include not handling negative numbers (take absolute values), infinite recursion if not reducing properly, or using slow naive methods. The real code pattern uses recursion or a while loop.

examples.trace: Compute gcd(48, 18): 48 % 18 = 12, so gcd(18, 12). 18 % 12 = 6, so gcd(12, 6). 12 % 6 = 0, so gcd(6, 0). Since b=0, return a=6. Final GCD=6.
Compute gcd(17, 13): 17 % 13 = 4, so gcd(13, 4). 13 % 4 = 1, so gcd(4, 1). 4 % 1 = 0, so gcd(1, 0). Since b=0, return a=1. Final GCD=1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Over All Pairs in a List
ladders.desc: Generate all unique pairs (i, j) from a list where i < j.
ladders.explanation: This level involves the fundamental operation of iterating over all unique pairs of elements in a list, which is needed for checking pairwise properties like coprimality. The intuition is to use nested loops: the outer loop goes from index 0 to n-2, and the inner loop goes from i+1 to n-1, ensuring each pair is considered exactly once without repetition or order. The algorithm simply loops through indices and accesses elements at those positions. Time complexity is O(n²) for a list of size n, and space complexity is O(1) as it only uses indices. This connects to higher levels by being part of the subset verification process. Common mistakes include including pairs where i ≥ j, leading to duplicates or self-pairs, or off-by-one errors in loop bounds. The real code pattern uses for loops with range.

examples.trace: List: [2, 3, 5], n=3. Outer loop i=0: inner loop j=1 to 2: pair (2,3), (2,5). Outer loop i=1: inner loop j=2 to 2: pair (3,5). Outer loop i=2: skip as i=2, n-1=2, so i < n-1 is false. All pairs generated: (2,3), (2,5), (3,5).
List: [1], n=1. Outer loop i=0: i < n-1? 0 < 0 is false, so no pairs generated. Output empty list.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Subsets and Bitmask Basics
ladders.desc: Learn what subsets are and how to represent them using binary numbers (bitmasks).
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces the concept of subsets and their representation using bitmasks, which is prerequisite for the brute force approach. A subset of a set is any selection of elements, including the empty set and the set itself. For an array of n elements, there are 2^n possible subsets. Bitmask representation uses an n-bit binary number where each bit corresponds to an element: 1 means include, 0 means exclude. For example, for array [a, b, c], bitmask 101 (binary) represents subset [a, c]. The intuition is that counting from 0 to 2^n - 1 in binary covers all combinations. This concept connects to higher levels by enabling efficient subset enumeration. Common mistakes include misunderstanding that subsets are unordered, or confusing bitmask with index. The real code pattern involves understanding binary numbers and bitwise operations.

examples.trace: Array has 2 elements, so 2^2=4 subsets. Represent with 2-bit masks: 00 → [], 01 → [x], 10 → [y], 11 → [x, y]. Each mask: bit0 for x, bit1 for y.
Array has 1 element, so 2^1=2 subsets. Masks: 0 → [], 1 → [p]. Bit0 for p.

testCases.input: Array: [a, b, c, d], how many subsets?
Array: [x, y, z], bitmask 110 represents which subset?
Array: [7, 8, 9], what subset does bitmask 000 represent?
Array: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5], how many bits in mask?

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Prime Masks O(n * 2^p)
approaches.summary: Represent each number by a bitmask of its prime factors, use DP to count subsets where masks do not overlap (indicating coprimality). Effective when numbers have small prime factors, limiting distinct primes p.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Coprime Subsets Using Prime Mask DP
ladders.desc: Given an array of integers, count all subsets where every pair of numbers is coprime (gcd=1) using dynamic programming with prime factor bitmasks.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that two numbers are coprime if they share no common prime factors. We can represent each number as a bitmask where each bit corresponds to a distinct prime factor across all numbers. For example, if the distinct primes are [2,3,5], then 6=2*3 would have mask 110 (bits for 2 and 3 set). A subset is coprime if the bitwise OR of all masks has no overlapping bits (i.e., each prime appears at most once). We use DP where dp[mask] counts subsets whose combined prime mask equals 'mask'. We iterate through numbers, and for each number with mask 'm', we update dp: for each existing mask 'prev', if (prev & m) == 0 (no overlap), then dp[prev | m] += dp[prev]. Initialize dp[0]=1 (empty subset). The answer is sum of all dp[mask] except dp[0] (to exclude empty subset if required, but typically included). Time: O(n * 2^p) where p is number of distinct primes (≤ number of bits in mask, typically small). Space: O(2^p). Key steps: 1) Identify all distinct primes up to max number. 2) Map each prime to a bit index. 3) Compute mask for each number. 4) Run DP. 5) Sum results. Common mistakes: Not handling numbers with duplicate primes (e.g., 4=2*2) correctly—mask should only set bit once per prime. Also, ensure modulo operations if counts are large. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into the complete solution.

examples.trace: Distinct primes: [2,3,5], map to bits 0,1,2. Masks: 2→001, 3→010, 5→100. DP init: dp[000]=1. After processing all three numbers, dp has one way for each mask 000,001,010,011,100,101,110,111. Summing all masks (including dp[000] for empty subset) gives 8.

Distinct primes: [2,3,5], bits 0,1,2. Masks: 6=2*3→011, 10=2*5→101, 15=3*5→110. DP init: dp[000]=1. Process 6: m=011, update dp[011]=1. Process 10: m=101, prev masks: 000→dp[101]=1, 011→(011&101=001≠0)→skip (overlap on prime 2). Process 15: m=110, prev masks: 000→dp[110]=1, 011→(011&110=010≠0)→skip, 101→(101&110=100≠0)→skip. Sum dp except dp[000]: dp[011]=1 (subset {6}), dp[101]=1 ({10}), dp[110]=1 ({15}), total 3, plus empty subset {} counted as 1 if included, but problem typically excludes it, so output 3? Wait, check: subsets: {} (excluded), {6}, {10}, {15}, {6,10} not coprime (share 2), {6,15} not (share 3), {10,15} not (share 5), {6,10,15} not. So only 3 non-empty subsets. But example says 4, maybe including empty? Let's recalc: dp[000]=1 for empty, sum all dp: 1+1+1+1=4. So output 4 includes empty subset. Yes, typical problem counts all subsets including empty. So answer 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Update with Prime Masks
ladders.desc: Implement the dynamic programming transition that counts subsets by combining non-overlapping prime masks.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the solution. Given an array of prime masks for numbers, we maintain a DP array dp where dp[mask] represents the number of subsets whose combined prime mask is exactly 'mask'. We iterate through each number's mask 'm'. For each existing mask 'prev' in dp (we iterate over all masks from 0 to 2^p-1), if (prev & m) == 0 (meaning no common prime factors), then we can add the current number to subsets represented by 'prev', forming a new subset with mask 'prev | m'. So, dp[prev | m] += dp[prev]. We must process masks in reverse order to avoid double-counting the same number multiple times in one iteration (similar to knapsack DP). Initialize dp[0] = 1 for the empty subset. After processing all numbers, dp[mask] gives counts for each combined mask. The total coprime subsets is sum of all dp[mask] (including empty). Intuition: We are building subsets incrementally, ensuring at each step that adding a new number doesn't introduce shared primes with the existing subset. Time: O(n * 2^p) for nested loops. Space: O(2^p). Connection to L0: This DP update is the main loop in the full solution. Common mistakes: Not using reverse iteration for masks, leading to overcounting; or missing the bitwise AND check for overlap. Real code pattern: dp = [0]*(1<<p); dp[0]=1; for m in masks: for prev in range((1<<p)-1, -1, -1): if dp[prev] and (prev & m)==0: dp[prev|m] += dp[prev].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Representing Numbers as Prime Factor Bitmasks
ladders.desc: Convert each number into a bitmask where each bit represents a distinct prime factor, enabling efficient overlap checks.
ladders.explanation: The central technique is to map each number to a bitmask based on its prime factors. First, identify all distinct prime numbers that appear in the entire array (up to the maximum value). Let p be the count of these primes. Assign each prime a unique bit index (e.g., the smallest prime gets bit 0, next bit 1, etc.). For each number, compute its prime factorization. For each prime factor, set the corresponding bit in the mask to 1. If a number has repeated prime factors (e.g., 4=2*2), still set the bit only once because sharing the same prime multiple times still violates coprimality. The mask is an integer where bit i is 1 if prime i divides the number. For example, if primes are [2,3,5] with indices 0,1,2, then 6=2*3 has mask 011 (binary), which is 3 in decimal. Two numbers are coprime if their masks have no common set bits, i.e., (mask1 & mask2) == 0. This reduces the coprime check to a bitwise AND operation, which is O(1). Intuition: By encoding prime factors into bits, we transform a number theory problem into a bitmask combinatorial problem, leveraging fast bit operations. Time to compute masks: O(n * sqrt(max_val)) for factorization, but can be optimized with precomputed primes. Space: O(n) for storing masks. Connection to L1: These masks are input to the DP update routine. Common mistakes: Not handling number 1 correctly—1 has no prime factors, so its mask is 0. Also, ensuring the mask fits within p bits (p is typically small, e.g., ≤10 for numbers up to 1000). Real code pattern: primes = list of distinct primes; prime_to_bit = {p:i for i,p in enumerate(primes)}; masks = []; for num in nums: mask=0; for prime in primes: if num%prime==0: mask |= (1<<prime_to_bit[prime]); while num%prime==0: num//=prime; if num>1: handle large prime > sqrt(original) if needed.

examples.trace: Primes: 2→bit0, 3→bit1, 5→bit2. For 6: factors 2 and 3. Set bit0 and bit1: mask = 011 binary = 3 decimal. For 10: factors 2 and 5. Set bit0 and bit2: mask = 101 binary = 5 decimal. For 15: factors 3 and 5. Set bit1 and bit2: mask = 110 binary = 6 decimal.
Primes: only 2→bit0. For 1: no factors, mask=0. For 2: factor 2, set bit0: mask=1. For 4: factors 2 (only once in mask), set bit0: mask=1. For 8: factor 2, set bit0: mask=1. All masks are 0 or 1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Prime Factorization and Distinct Prime Collection
ladders.desc: Factorize numbers into prime factors and collect all distinct primes across the array.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves two steps: 1) Prime factorization of a number: breaking it down into its prime factors. For example, 12 = 2^2 * 3, so prime factors are 2 and 3. We only need the distinct primes, not their exponents, for the mask. 2) Collecting all distinct primes from all numbers in the array. This gives us the set of primes to map to bits. To factorize, iterate from 2 to sqrt(n). For each i, if n%i==0, then i is a prime factor (since we divide out all smaller primes first). Add i to the set, and divide n by i repeatedly until n%i!=0. After the loop, if n>1, then n is a prime factor (for primes larger than sqrt(original)). Collect primes for each number into a global set. Intuition: Knowing the primes allows us to define the bitmask space; factorization extracts the relevant primes per number. Time: O(n * sqrt(max_val)) worst-case, but efficient for small numbers. Space: O(p) for the prime set. Connection to L2: This provides the primes and factorization needed to compute masks. Common mistakes: Not handling the case where n remains >1 after loop (it's a prime); or inefficiently checking all numbers up to n instead of sqrt(n). Real code pattern: primes=set(); for num in nums: n=num; i=2; while i*i<=n: if n%i==0: primes.add(i); while n%i==0: n//=i; i+=1; if n>1: primes.add(n). Then sort primes for consistent bit assignment.

examples.trace: For 1: n=1, loop skipped, n=1 not >1, so no prime. For 4: n=4, i=2, 4%2=0→add 2, divide 4/2=2, 2%2=0→divide 2/2=1, exit loop, n=1 not >1. For 9: n=9, i=2, 9%2≠0, i=3, 9%3=0→add 3, divide 9/3=3, 3%3=0→divide 3/3=1, exit loop, n=1 not >1. Primes: {2,3}, sort→[2,3].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bitwise AND and OR for Overlap Checking
ladders.desc: Use bitwise operations to check if two sets of prime factors intersect and to combine them.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves using bitwise AND (&) to test for overlap between two bitmasks, and bitwise OR (|) to combine masks. Given two masks A and B (integers where each bit represents a prime factor), the bitwise AND A & B results in a mask where bits are set only if they are set in both A and B. If A & B == 0, then no bits overlap, meaning the two numbers share no common prime factors—they are coprime. The bitwise OR A | B results in a mask where bits are set if they are set in either A or B, representing the union of prime factors. For example, if primes are [2,3,5] with bits 0,1,2, and A=011 (bits 0 and 1 set for primes 2 and 3), B=101 (bits 0 and 2 set for primes 2 and 5), then A & B = 001 (bit 0 set, indicating overlap on prime 2), so they are not coprime. A | B = 111 (all bits set). Intuition: Bitwise operations are extremely fast (O(1)) and allow efficient set operations on small sets of primes. This is key to the DP approach where we repeatedly check and combine masks. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: These operations are applied to masks derived from prime factorization. Common mistakes: Confusing bitwise AND with logical AND; or not using parentheses correctly in expressions. Real code pattern: overlap = (mask1 & mask2) == 0; combined = mask1 | mask2.

examples.trace: A=3 in binary: 011 (bits for primes 2 and 3). B=5 in binary: 101 (bits for primes 2 and 5). Compute A&B: bitwise AND: 011 & 101 = 001 (decimal 1), since only bit0 is set in both. This indicates overlap on prime 2. Compute A|B: bitwise OR: 011 | 101 = 111 (decimal 7), all bits set.

A=2 in binary: 010 (bit for prime 3). B=4 in binary: 100 (bit for prime 5). Compute A&B: 010 & 100 = 000 (decimal 0), no overlap. Compute A|B: 010 | 100 = 110 (decimal 6), bits for primes 3 and 5 set.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Prime Numbers and Coprimality
ladders.desc: Understand what prime numbers are and when two numbers are coprime (gcd=1).
ladders.explanation: Prime numbers are integers greater than 1 that have no positive divisors other than 1 and themselves. Examples: 2, 3, 5, 7, 11. Every integer can be uniquely expressed as a product of primes (prime factorization). For example, 12 = 2^2 * 3. Two numbers are coprime (or relatively prime) if their greatest common divisor (GCD) is 1, meaning they share no common prime factors. For example, 8 (2^3) and 9 (3^2) are coprime because they have no primes in common. In contrast, 6 (2*3) and 9 (3^2) are not coprime because they share prime 3. To check coprimality, you can compute GCD using Euclidean algorithm: gcd(a,b) = gcd(b, a mod b) until b=0. If gcd=1, they are coprime. Intuition: Coprimality is a fundamental number theory concept used in many algorithms. In our problem, we count subsets where all pairs are coprime. Time to check coprimality: O(log min(a,b)) for GCD. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This concept underlies why bitwise overlap checking works—no common primes means coprime. Common mistakes: Thinking 1 is prime (it's not); or confusing coprime with both numbers being prime (e.g., 2 and 3 are coprime and prime, but 4 and 9 are coprime without being prime). Real code pattern: def gcd(a,b): while b: a,b = b, a%b; return a; def coprime(a,b): return gcd(a,b)==1.

examples.trace: Prime factors: 8=2^3, 9=3^2. No common primes. Compute GCD: gcd(8,9): 8%9=8, gcd(9,8); 9%8=1, gcd(8,1); 8%1=0, gcd(1,0)→gcd=1. So coprime.
Prime factors: 12=2^2*3, 18=2*3^2. Common primes: 2 and 3. Compute GCD: gcd(12,18): 12%18=12, gcd(18,12); 18%12=6, gcd(12,6); 12%6=0, gcd(6,0)→gcd=6. Not 1, so not coprime.
